course: CMU 21-254: Linear Algebra and Vector Calculus for Engineers
title: Vectors, Dot Products, Magnitudes, and Angles
audience: SI leader teaching live on a whiteboard
toneRule: Keep explanations very simple, verbal, and board-friendly. Avoid textbook phrasing unless needed.
sessionGoal: Students should be able to do basic vector computations and explain what the dot product says about direction.
quickStartIfUnprepared: Remind students that vectors have size and direction.
Do Problem 1 to warm up components.
Do Problem 3 to connect dot product with perpendicular vectors.
sayThisFirst: A vector has size and direction.
The dot product gives a number, not another vector.
The dot product tells us how much two vectors point in the same direction.
If two vectors have dot product zero, they are perpendicular.
For points A and B, the vector from A to B is \(\overrightarrow{AB}=B-A\). Order matters.

boardPlan.whatToSay: These four formulas are enough for today. Most mistakes are notation or arithmetic, not concept.
Start with component arithmetic. Make them say out loud whether the answer should be a vector or a number.
Emphasize that \(\overrightarrow{AB}\) means start at A and end at B, so we do B minus A.
Use the dot product condition for perpendicular vectors. Then normalize the vector.
Ask students to name the three most important mistakes: wrong order for \(\overrightarrow{AB}\), forgetting squares in magnitude, and thinking dot product gives a vector.

keyFormulas.name: Magnitude
keyFormulas.plainEnglish: Magnitude is the length of the vector.

keyFormulas.name: Angle formula
keyFormulas.plainEnglish: This connects dot product to the angle between vectors.

keyFormulas.name: Vector from A to B
keyFormulas.plainEnglish: Endpoint minus start point. Order matters.

keyFormulas.name: Unit vector
keyFormulas.plainEnglish: Divide a nonzero vector by its length.

commonMistakes: Writing \(AB\) when you mean \(\overrightarrow{AB}\). Use the arrow.
Forgetting to square every component when finding magnitude.
Thinking the dot product gives a vector. It gives a scalar.
Saying vectors are perpendicular because they look perpendicular. Check the dot product.

notationWarnings.reason: AB usually names a segment or distance. The arrow tells students this is the vector from A to B.

problems.title: Component arithmetic and dot product
problems.studentPromptLatex: Let \(u=\langle 3,-1,2\rangle\) and \(v=\langle -2,4,1\rangle\). Find \(2u-v\), compute \(u\cdot v\), find \(\|u\|\), and decide whether \(u\) and \(v\) are perpendicular.
problems.whatToSayBefore: Ask: which parts of this problem give vectors, and which parts give numbers?

boardSketchInstructions: Draw a small 3-column component table for x, y, z.
Put u and v in the first two rows.
Circle the fact that dot product is a scalar.
visualSpec.accuracyNote: This is an exact component table, not a geometric sketch.

walkthrough.label: Scale \(u\)
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Scalar multiplication just multiplies every component.

walkthrough.label: Subtract \(v\)
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Be careful with the minus sign on the first component: 6 minus negative 2 is 8.

walkthrough.label: Compute the dot product
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: The dot product is a number.

walkthrough.label: Check perpendicular
walkthrough.latex: u\cdot v=-8\ne 0,\quad \text{so }u\text{ and }v\text{ are not perpendicular.}
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Perpendicular would mean dot product exactly zero.

commonMistakes: Stopping at \(u\cdot v=-8\) and saying perpendicular. It must be zero.

practiceLabPointer.message: For more practice, use Practice Lab Section 1 and look for vector operation problems.

problems.title: From two points to a direction vector
skills: Vector from point A to point B
problems.studentPromptLatex: Let \(A=(1,-2,3)\) and \(B=(5,0,-1)\). Find \(\overrightarrow{AB}\), find the distance from \(A\) to \(B\), and find a unit vector pointing from \(A\) to \(B\).
problems.whatToSayBefore: The phrase 'from A to B' tells us the order: endpoint minus start point.

visualSpec.purpose: Show the vector from A to B as an arrow with the correct direction.
boardSketchInstructions: Mark A and B roughly, but do not spend too long on exact scale.
Draw an arrow starting at A and ending at B.
Write 'endpoint minus start point' next to the sketch.
visualSpec.accuracyNote: The coordinates and vector values are exact. The whiteboard drawing can be rough, but the arrow direction must be from A to B.

walkthrough.label: Use endpoint minus start point
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Start at A, end at B, so use B minus A.

walkthrough.label: Subtract component by component
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: This is the direction and displacement from A to B.

walkthrough.label: Distance is the length of the vector
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: Distance between the points is the magnitude of the displacement vector.

walkthrough.label: Divide by the length to get a unit vector
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: A unit vector keeps the same direction but has length 1.

commonMistakes: Using \(A-B\), which points from B to A instead.
Forgetting to divide every component by 6 when making the unit vector.

practiceLabPointer.message: For more practice, use Practice Lab Section 1 and look for point-to-vector or magnitude problems.

problems.title: Choose a parameter to make vectors perpendicular
problems.studentPromptLatex: Let \(u=\langle 2,1\rangle\) and \(v=\langle k,8\rangle\). Find \(k\) so that \(u\) and \(v\) are perpendicular. Then find a unit vector in the direction of \(v\).
problems.whatToSayBefore: When a problem says perpendicular, immediately think dot product equals zero.

visualSpec.purpose: Show the exact final vectors after solving for k.
boardSketchInstructions: After solving, draw \(v=\langle -4,8\rangle\) from the origin.
Mark a right-angle symbol between the two directions.
visualSpec.accuracyNote: These two vectors are exactly perpendicular because \((2)(-4)+(1)(8)=0\).

walkthrough.label: Use the perpendicular condition
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: This is the main idea of the problem.

walkthrough.label: Find the length of \(v\)
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: We need the length before we can make a unit vector.

walkthrough.label: Divide by the length
walkthrough.sayOutLoud: This is the same direction as v, just length 1.

commonMistakes: Trying to use a picture before setting up the dot product.
Forgetting to plug \(k=-4\) back into \(v\) before finding the unit vector.

practiceLabPointer.message: For more practice, use Practice Lab Section 1 and search for dot product or unit vector.

wrapUpQuestions: What does the dot product output: a scalar or a vector?
What dot product value means perpendicular?
What is the correct formula for the vector from A to B?
How do you turn a vector into a unit vector?

linksToWebsite.label: Practice Lab Section 1
linksToWebsite.description: Use this section for more vector, matrix, transpose, and basic operation practice.

qualityControlNotes: All uses of vector AB should be written as \(\overrightarrow{AB}\).
All dot product results in this session are scalars.
Problem 3 was independently checked: k=-4 makes \(\langle2,1\rangle\cdot\langle-4,8\rangle=0\).
Visuals should be rendered as teaching sketches. Exact coordinates are provided in visualSpec; board drawings may be not to scale unless explicitly marked exact.
reviewSummary: Notation checked: vector from A to B is consistently written as \(\overrightarrow{AB}=B-A\), not plain AB.
Arithmetic checked for all three problems.
Visual specs are exact where possible; 3D point sketch is labeled rough/not-to-scale while coordinate values remain exact.
Explanations are intentionally simple and whiteboard-friendly.
